text: portions of his coding that had become irrational with fear. If Newton meant them harm, what was to be gained by reactivating Ronin and leading him here? Why bother powering him back on if he had malicious intentions ? He gently passed Lara’s limp form into Newton’s outstretched arms. His limbs locked as he pulled away. She was so fragile. Even losing a few seconds of contact, when they were potentially so limited a commodity, made him doubt if this was the right thing to do . Holding her now will not help her recover . Finally, he withdrew his hands, quickly turning to the hatch. He lowered himself onto the ladder without further hesitation. The sound of his boots on the rungs echoed through the corridor, which stretched into total darkness in either direction . Ronin turned his attention upward the instant his feet were on the bottom . Newton stood over the opening, maneuvering slowly to guide Lara’s legs through. He dropped into a crouch as he lowered her, adjusting his hold so his arms were beneath her armpits. Despite the pain she should have felt at such handling, she remained unconscious, head lolling . Reaching up, Ronin placed his hands on her hips and accepted her weight, drawing her against him with his cheek pressed to her bruised midsection. He was as gentle as possible; her body had been so battered, it seemed there wasn’t an inch of her without injury . “I have her .” Newton released Lara. Ronin shifted as her torso sagged, preventing her weight from coming down on her damaged ribs or broken arm. Adjusting his hold, he cradled her against his chest, smoothing her hair out of her face . Bare metal feet clanked onto the ladder. Newton closed the latch as quietly as it had been opened, plunging the tunnel into darkness . Ronin’s optics switched to night-vision. The walls and floor were concrete, the latter coated in a fine layer of dust. Were those brush strokes? There were no footprints or other signs of habitation, but the dust didn’t appear to have settled naturally . “This way,” Newton said, voice low . They proceeded down the corridor, Newton slightly ahead, footfalls scraping lightly over the dirt-blanketed floor. There were light fixtures high on the walls every fifteen feet, but no bulbs within their wire coverings. The hints of paint on the walls were too faded to decipher . Each step heightened Ronin’s anxiety. He was carrying his beloved down a pitch-black corridor with nowhere to run, no weapons, no cover. If there was a trap waiting ahead, it would be unforgivingly effective. An increasingly strong thought-chain posited there was nothing here to help her. There was nothing in all the Dust that could help her. This place would be Lara’s tomb . Newton slowed, glancing over his shoulder. “Nearly there. Just around here .” The corridor continued ahead, farther than Ronin’s optics could see in the dark, but Newton turned into an opening just ahead. Ronin followed close behind . His vision blazed white as he was hit by a bright light. Twisting away from it, he shielded Lara’s body with his . A trap, after all . “Don’t move!” someone shouted . “Quite reassuring to see security has not grown lax,” Newton said from beside Ronin . “Are you two bots , too ?” “I am,” replied Ronin, turning his head toward the man. With his night vision switched off, humanoid silhouettes were clear against the bright spotlight, all gathered behind a waist-high barrier with firearms pointed at Ronin, Newton, and Lara. “The woman is human .” “What’s wrong with her ?” “She was beaten near to death by the self-titled Warlord of Cheyenne,” Newton said . The people behind the barricade whispered to each other for several moments . “Did he follow you ?” “No.” Ronin paused, looking down to Lara. “As far as he knows, he ended us. Left us for dead .” The spotlight shifted, and the man who’d been speaking turned to two of the other figures. “Secure the perimeter .” The pair hurried past Ronin — soldiers, dressed in matching uniforms — and down the perpendicular corridor . “If you have any weapons,” the leader said, “place them on the ground . Now .” “We’re unarmed,” Ronin replied, “and we’re out of time. If you can’t help her, tell me now, so I can find someone who can before it’s too late .” “Turn around. Slowly .” It wasn’t a request, and that made it no easier to comply. Exposing Lara to potential hostiles went against Ronin’s driving need to protect her. All the same, disobeying was likely to accelerate their mutual ends . He turned, bringing the barricade into full view. His optics blurred and focused repeatedly, battling to balance the contrast between the overwhelming light and the thick darkness behind it . “Garrison, Walker, take the girl to the infirmary,” the leader commanded . Two figures advanced, features clarifying as they neared. Both held automatic rifles, similar to the one Ronin had carried. Their uniforms matched the soldiers who went into the corridor — much repaired, camouflage faded, but well-kept nonetheless. The men slung their rifles over their shoulders . “I’ll carry her.” Ronin stepped back . The soldiers paused, hands drifting toward their weapons . “You’re in no position to make demands,” the leader said, and he, too, stepped into the light. He was a middle-aged man with short-cropped hair, double bars displayed at the center of his chest. A captain. The shred of information was from Ronin’s old life. A tiny bit of data reclaimed after so many decades . For nine seconds, Ronin and the Captain stared at one another, neither moving. Walker and Garrison stood uncertainly . “They won’t hurt her,” Newton said . “How do I know?” Ronin demanded. “How do I know they’ll take care of her ?” “We have the facilities to help her,” the
